expected_output.title: RELATING TO INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY SERVICES AND CYBERSECURITY
expected_output.summary: House Bill No. 4 (Idaho, 2025) updates Idaho law to strengthen statewide cybersecurity governance by expanding the powers and duties of the Office of Information Technology Services (OITS). It requires OITS to ensure state agencies implement and maintain cybersecurity best practices, coordinate and direct agencies on information security needs, conduct penetration testing and vulnerability scanning to identify and mitigate risks, and ensure mandatory cybersecurity education and training for state employees, along with maintaining a statewide cybersecurity best-practices repository. The bill also mandates the use of multifactor identification (defined as using two or more credential types such as knowledge-, possession-, or inherence-based credentials) for access to state IT devices and services—covering systems like email, cloud storage, web applications, networks, databases, and servers—and extends the same multifactor requirement to the legislative and judicial branches and elected constitutional officers (and their staffs) via a new code section. The act includes an emergency clause and takes effect July 1, 2025.

input.prompt: Extract legislative metadata from this bill.

expected_output.title: Idaho Lottery Bulk Purchase Prohibition Act
expected_output.summary: Idaho House Bill No. 504 — introduced by the State Affairs Committee — amends the Idaho State Lottery statutes to prohibit "bulk purchase transactions," defined as any purchase of lottery tickets exceeding $5,000 within a 24-hour period by any person or persons "acting in concert," whether in a single or multiple transactions and at one or multiple locations. The bill grants the Idaho State Lottery the authority to deny prize payments on tickets tied to such transactions, investigate suspicious purchase patterns using evidence such as video surveillance and transaction data, and penalize any licensed retailer who knowingly facilitates a bulk purchase — up to and including termination of their contract. The bill also makes several technical corrections and section redesignations to existing lottery statutes, and takes effect on July 1, 2026.

expected_output.title:
To the President of the United States, the Senate and the House of Representatives of the United States in Congress Assembled, the Congressional Delegation Representing the State of
  Idaho in the Congress of the United States, the Secretary of the United States Department of Agriculture, and the Secretary of the United States Department of Health and Human Services.
expected_output.summary:
House Joint Memorial No. 10 (2026) is a non-binding resolution from the Idaho Legislature urging the federal government to ban six FDA-approved artificial sweeteners — aspartame, acesulfame potassium, advantame,      
  neotame, saccharin, and sucralose — from all federally administered school meal programs, including the National School Lunch Program and School Breakfast Program. The memorial cites insufficient long-term research on
   artificial sweeteners' effects on children's development and metabolism, and advocates for the use of real sugar as a natural alternative. It also highlights Idaho's economic interest as the nation's second-largest  
  sugar beet producer, with the industry contributing over $1 billion annually to the state economy. Sponsored by the House Agricultural Affairs Committee.

input.prompt: Extract legislative metadata from this bill.

expected_output.title:
An Act Relating to Education — amending Sections 33-1802, 33-1804, 33-1805, 33-1806, 33-1807, 33-1809, 33-1811, and 33-1002, Idaho Code, and adding new Section 33-1809A, to revise provisions regarding literacy        
  definitions, the Idaho Comprehensive Literacy Plan, student reading instruction and intervention, reading and literacy assessment, the literacy intervention program, accountability and continuous improvement, 
  additional literacy intervention and school literacy plans, dyslexia, and summative reading assessments; and declaring an emergency.
expected_output.summary:
Idaho House Bill 383 (2025) is an emergency education reform bill that overhauls the state's K-3 literacy framework to align with the science of reading. It bans three-cueing instructional methods, introduces new     
  definitions (including "science of reading" and "independent literacy expert"), and transitions from a single statewide reading assessment to a system where LEAs choose from 3+ state-vetted summative assessments. The
  bill creates a new intervention process where underperforming elementary schools are paired with independent literacy experts for 3-year improvement plans, strengthens dyslexia screening and educator training         
  requirements, and revises literacy funding to weight student reading growth and economically disadvantaged students. Effective July 1, 2025.

input.prompt: Extract legislative metadata from this bill.

expected_output.title:
A Joint Resolution proposing an amendment to Section 8, Article IX of the Constitution of the State of Idaho, relating to state trust lands; stating the question to be submitted to the electorate;       
  directing the Legislative Council to prepare the statements required by law; and directing the Secretary of State to publish the amendment and arguments as required by law.
expected_output.summary:
Senate Joint Resolution No. 103 (2026) proposes a constitutional amendment to Article IX, Section 8 of the Idaho Constitution regarding state trust lands. It creates a new category of public lands — those granted to
  or acquired from the federal government (excluding original statehood land grants) — to be held in a separate, permanent "public lands trust." These lands could never be sold and could only be exchanged with
  two-thirds legislative approval. The State Board of Land Commissioners would manage them using best management practices that balance multiple uses, avoid permanent impairment, and promote public recreation, scenic
  values, watershed quality, and wildlife habitat. Revenue from these lands would go into a new Public Lands Trust Fund, appropriated first for land operations and then to compensate counties, improve public access, and
   support public education facilities. The amendment would be placed on the ballot at the next general election. Sponsored by the Senate State Affairs Committee.

sections_affected.raw_section: Section 8, Article IX, Constitution of the State of Idaho

input.prompt: Extract legislative metadata from this bill.

expected_output.title:
An Act relating to appropriations; reducing the appropriation for fiscal year 2026; reducing the number of authorized full-time equivalent positions for fiscal year 2026; transferring moneys from the    
  Public School Income Fund to the General Fund for fiscal year 2026; and declaring an emergency.
expected_output.summary:
Senate Bill 1331 (2026) is an emergency appropriations reduction bill that cuts $192,656,100 across virtually every branch and agency of Idaho state government for fiscal year 2026 (July 1, 2025 - June 30, 2026).
  Sponsored by the Senate Finance Committee, the bill reduces previously enacted FY2026 appropriations from the Laws of 2025 across 31 major budget areas including public schools ($22.4M), universities and colleges
  ($19.5M+), Health and Welfare/Medicaid ($75M+), Corrections ($7.2M+), State Police ($2.4M+), Environmental Quality ($1.5M), and dozens of other agencies. It also reduces approximately 97 FTE positions across 25+
  programs and transfers $22,366,500 from the Public School Income Fund to the General Fund. The cuts span personnel costs ($48.6M), operating expenditures ($20.5M), capital outlay ($1.9M), and trustee/benefit payments
  ($121.6M). Effective immediately upon passage.
